arguments.name: fix-serialization-batch
arguments.prompt:
Fix AOT warnings in serialization files in the project at /tmp/sv-f731aab8db3348d48d6b70e7fd037449/before.

The goal is to replace all `JsonSerializer.Serialize` and `JsonSerializer.Deserialize` calls in .Serialization.cs files with AOT-safe alternatives. Here are the exact replacements needed:

## For IJsonModel types - use static Deserialize methods and WriteObjectValue

### SystemData deserialization (most common pattern - in ALL these files):
Replace:
```csharp
systemData = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<SystemData>(property.Value.GetRawText());
```
With:
```csharp
systemData = SystemData.DeserializeSystemData(property.Value);
```
Files: ManagementGroupData.Serialization.cs, ManagementGroupSubscriptionData.Serialization.cs, DataPolicyManifestData.Serialization.cs, DescendantData.Serialization.cs, FeatureData.Serialization.cs, ManagementLockData.Serialization.cs, PolicyDefinitionData.Serialization.cs, PolicySetDefinitionData.Serialization.cs, ResourceGroupData.Serialization.cs, TagResourceData.Serialization.cs, GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs, PolicyAssignmentData.Serialization.cs, EntityData.Serialization.cs, TrackedResourceExtendedData.Serialization.cs

### JsonElement serialization:
Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, document.RootElement);
```
With:
```csharp
document.RootElement.WriteTo(writer);
```
Files containing this pattern: GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs, ResourceGroupExportResult.Serialization.cs, PolicyDefinitionData.Serialization.cs, PolicySetDefinitionData.Serialization.cs

### GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs specific fixes:
File path: Resources/Generated/GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Plan)` (line ~45) with `writer.WriteObjectValue(Plan)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Identity)` (line ~77) with `writer.WriteObjectValue(Identity)`  
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ArmPlan>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ArmPlan.DeserializeArmPlan(property.Value)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentity>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ManagedServiceIdentity.DeserializeManagedServiceIdentity(property.Value)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ExtendedLocation>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ExtendedLocation.DeserializeExtendedLocation(property.Value)`
(SystemData already handled above)

### TrackedResourceExtendedData.Serialization.cs:
File path: Resources/Generated/Models/TrackedResourceExtendedData.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ExtendedLocation)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(ExtendedLocation)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ExtendedLocation>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ExtendedLocation.DeserializeExtendedLocation(property.Value)`
(SystemData already handled above)

### PolicyAssignmentData.Serialization.cs:
File path: Resources/Generated/PolicyAssignmentData.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedIdentity)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(ManagedIdentity)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentity>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ManagedServiceIdentity.DeserializeManagedServiceIdentity(property.Value)` 
(SystemData already handled above)

Also fix ANY `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, document.RootElement)` in this file with `document.RootElement.WriteTo(writer)`.

### PolicyAssignmentPatch.Serialization.cs:
File path: Resources/Generated/Models/PolicyAssignmentPatch.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Identity)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(Identity)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentity>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ManagedServiceIdentity.DeserializeManagedServiceIdentity(property.Value)`

Also fix ANY `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, document.RootElement)` in this file with `document.RootElement.WriteTo(writer)`.

### EntityData.Serialization.cs:
File path: ManagementGroup/Generated/Models/EntityData.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Parent)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(Parent)` (Parent is SubResource)
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<SubResource>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `SubResource.DeserializeSubResource(property.Value)`
(SystemData already handled above)

### ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs:
File path: Common/Custom/Models/ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs

Line 31: Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)` with:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type"u8);
writer.WriteStringValue(ManagedServiceIdentityType.ToString());
```

Line 49: Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(item.Value)`

Line 206: Replace `type = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}");` with `type = new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString());`

Line 218: Replace `dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText()));` with `dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), UserAssignedIdentity.DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(property0.Value));`

### OperationStatusResult.Serialization.cs:
File path: Common/Generated/Models/OperationStatusResult.Serialization.cs

Line 84: Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error)` with `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error, ResourceManagerJsonContext.Default.ResponseError)` — ResponseError is external so we must use a source-generated context.

Line 173: Replace `array.Add(JsonSerializer.Deserialize<OperationStatusResult>(item.GetRawText()))` with `array.Add(DeserializeOperationStatusResult(item))`

Line 184: Replace `error = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `error = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText(), ResourceManagerJsonContext.Default.ResponseError)`

Make sure to add `using Azure.ResourceManager.Serialization;` to any file that uses `ResourceManagerJsonContext` if it's not already present.

### ResourceGroupExportResult.Serialization.cs:
File path: Resources/Generated/Models/ResourceGroupExportResult.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error)` with `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error, ResourceManagerJsonContext.Default.ResponseError)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText(), ResourceManagerJsonContext.Default.ResponseError)`
(JsonElement serialization already handled above)

Make sure to add `using Azure.ResourceManager.Serialization;` if needed.

## Important notes:
- Use the `edit` tool for all changes (not sed or bash text manipulation)
- The `using Azure.ResourceManager.Models;` should already be present in most files for SystemData, ManagedServiceIdentity etc.
- For `WriteObjectValue`, no options argument is needed (it defaults to null which is fine)
- Do NOT touch any non-serialization patterns (like Bicep, ModelReaderWriter, etc.)
- After all edits, do NOT build - I will build separately

data.reasoningText: The `[JsonSerializerContext]` is wrong. It should be `[JsonSourceGenerationOptions]` or no class attribute at all - just `[JsonSerializable]` attributes. Let me fix the context class. In .NET 8+, you only need `[JsonSerializable]` attributes on a partial class inheriting from `JsonSerializerContext`. You don't use `[JsonSerializerContext]` as an attribute - you should only inherit from it. The source generator is triggered by the `[JsonSerializable]` attributes on a class that derives from `JsonSerializerContext`.

data.reasoningText:
Two issues:

1. `ResponseError` is not being source-generated. The source generator probably can't handle it because it's an external type without proper public API surface. Let me check what the actual generated property name would be, or if the source gen failed.

Actually, the error says `'ResourceManagerJsonContext' does not contain a definition for 'ResponseError'`. The source generator should generate a property named `ResponseError` for `[JsonSerializable(typeof(ResponseError))]`. But it seems it didn't. This could be because `ResponseError` is from Azure.Core and might have a custom converter that prevents source generation, or because the source generator produced a different property name.
 
I need to verify whether the source generator actually produced anything for `ResponseError`, or if it silently failed because the type isn't compatible with source generation—possibly because it lacks `IJsonModel` support. I'm wondering if the issue might just be a property naming mismatch or an accessibility problem with the ResponseError type itself. Let me verify whether ResponseError is actually accessible in this context.

OK so:
1. ResponseError has an internal `Converter` class that's not accessible, so source gen can't use it (SYSLIB1220). This means I can't source-gen ResponseError. I need to handle ResponseError differently - either write it manually or use a custom converter.

2. The ArmOperation IL2091 is because `Rehydrate<T>` has `NonPublicConstructors` but `GenericOperationSource<T>` needs `PublicConstructors | NonPublicConstructors`. I need to add `PublicConstructors` too.

3. The serialization files still have `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<SystemData>` - the background agent apparently hasn't finished or hasn't touched those yet. 

For ResponseError, I need to write a custom `JsonConverter<ResponseError>` manually since I can't source-gen it and it doesn't implement IJsonModel, or fall back to manual serialization with Utf8JsonWriter/Utf8JsonReader if needed. Let me check the structure of ResponseError to see what properties I'm working with.

Now I'm looking at the ResponseError structure—it has Code, Message, InnerError, Target, and Details properties. Since ResponseError has an internal converter that the JsonSerializer can discover at runtime but won't work with AOT, the best approach is to write a custom JsonConverter with manual Utf8JsonReader/Utf8JsonWriter logic and register it via JsonSourceGenerationOptions, though that's complex for all these nested properties. are actually being serialized. 

Looking at the call sites:
1. `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error)` - full serialization of ResponseError to writer
2. `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText())` - full deserialization from JSON string

Actually, ResponseError does have an internal converter that works with reflection. At runtime with standard serialization, it works fine. The issue is only with AOT. 

Since ResponseError's converter is internal to Azure.Core, I can't reference it in source-gen. But I CAN create a wrapper approach. Let me think...

Actually the simplest approach: since the ResponseError's internal converter works correctly at runtime with reflection, and the source generator just can't "see" it, I can create a custom `JsonConverter<ResponseError>` that manually serializes/deserializes. But that's complex and error-prone.

The real problem is that source gen can't instantiate `Azure.ResponseError.Converter` because it's internal, even though `JsonSerializer` at runtime can access it via reflection. So the existing code works fine at runtime but fails AOT analysis. Since I can't easily create a proper AOT-safe serializer for ResponseError without significant effort, and this serialization is internal anyway, I should look for a pragmatic workaround. Writing custom converter... I'm realizing that marking internal serialization methods with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` would propagate up the call chain and make everything trim-incompatible, which defeats the purpose. Let me reconsider—since `ResponseError` is from `Azure.Core` and has an internal converter that works but isn't accessible, maybe I can work around this differently by examining what options are actually available. I'm wondering if `ResponseError` in Azure.Core 1.46.1 might already implement `IJsonModel<ResponseError>`, which would give me a built-in serialization path without needing custom converters or reflection tricks. Let me check what's available in that specific version.

kind.prompt:
Fix AOT warnings in serialization files in the project at /tmp/sv-f731aab8db3348d48d6b70e7fd037449/before.

The goal is to replace all `JsonSerializer.Serialize` and `JsonSerializer.Deserialize` calls in .Serialization.cs files with AOT-safe alternatives. Here are the exact replacements needed:

## For IJsonModel types - use static Deserialize methods and WriteObjectValue

### SystemData deserialization (most common pattern - in ALL these files):
Replace:
```csharp
systemData = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<SystemData>(property.Value.GetRawText());
```
With:
```csharp
systemData = SystemData.DeserializeSystemData(property.Value);
```
Files: ManagementGroupData.Serialization.cs, ManagementGroupSubscriptionData.Serialization.cs, DataPolicyManifestData.Serialization.cs, DescendantData.Serialization.cs, FeatureData.Serialization.cs, ManagementLockData.Serialization.cs, PolicyDefinitionData.Serialization.cs, PolicySetDefinitionData.Serialization.cs, ResourceGroupData.Serialization.cs, TagResourceData.Serialization.cs, GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs, PolicyAssignmentData.Serialization.cs, EntityData.Serialization.cs, TrackedResourceExtendedData.Serialization.cs

### JsonElement serialization:
Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, document.RootElement);
```
With:
```csharp
document.RootElement.WriteTo(writer);
```
Files containing this pattern: GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs, ResourceGroupExportResult.Serialization.cs, PolicyDefinitionData.Serialization.cs, PolicySetDefinitionData.Serialization.cs

### GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs specific fixes:
File path: Resources/Generated/GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Plan)` (line ~45) with `writer.WriteObjectValue(Plan)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Identity)` (line ~77) with `writer.WriteObjectValue(Identity)`  
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ArmPlan>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ArmPlan.DeserializeArmPlan(property.Value)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentity>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ManagedServiceIdentity.DeserializeManagedServiceIdentity(property.Value)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ExtendedLocation>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ExtendedLocation.DeserializeExtendedLocation(property.Value)`
(SystemData already handled above)

### TrackedResourceExtendedData.Serialization.cs:
File path: Resources/Generated/Models/TrackedResourceExtendedData.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ExtendedLocation)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(ExtendedLocation)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ExtendedLocation>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ExtendedLocation.DeserializeExtendedLocation(property.Value)`
(SystemData already handled above)

### PolicyAssignmentData.Serialization.cs:
File path: Resources/Generated/PolicyAssignmentData.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedIdentity)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(ManagedIdentity)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentity>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ManagedServiceIdentity.DeserializeManagedServiceIdentity(property.Value)` 
(SystemData already handled above)

Also fix ANY `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, document.RootElement)` in this file with `document.RootElement.WriteTo(writer)`.

### PolicyAssignmentPatch.Serialization.cs:
File path: Resources/Generated/Models/PolicyAssignmentPatch.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Identity)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(Identity)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentity>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ManagedServiceIdentity.DeserializeManagedServiceIdentity(property.Value)`

Also fix ANY `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, document.RootElement)` in this file with `document.RootElement.WriteTo(writer)`.

### EntityData.Serialization.cs:
File path: ManagementGroup/Generated/Models/EntityData.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Parent)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(Parent)` (Parent is SubResource)
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<SubResource>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `SubResource.DeserializeSubResource(property.Value)`
(SystemData already handled above)

### ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs:
File path: Common/Custom/Models/ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs

Line 31: Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)` with:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type"u8);
writer.WriteStringValue(ManagedServiceIdentityType.ToString());
```

Line 49: Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions)` with `writer.WriteObjectValue(item.Value)`

Line 206: Replace `type = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}");` with `type = new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString());`

Line 218: Replace `dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText()));` with `dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), UserAssignedIdentity.DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(property0.Value));`

### OperationStatusResult.Serialization.cs:
File path: Common/Generated/Models/OperationStatusResult.Serialization.cs

Line 84: Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error)` with `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error, ResourceManagerJsonContext.Default.ResponseError)` — ResponseError is external so we must use a source-generated context.

Line 173: Replace `array.Add(JsonSerializer.Deserialize<OperationStatusResult>(item.GetRawText()))` with `array.Add(DeserializeOperationStatusResult(item))`

Line 184: Replace `error = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `error = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText(), ResourceManagerJsonContext.Default.ResponseError)`

Make sure to add `using Azure.ResourceManager.Serialization;` to any file that uses `ResourceManagerJsonContext` if it's not already present.

### ResourceGroupExportResult.Serialization.cs:
File path: Resources/Generated/Models/ResourceGroupExportResult.Serialization.cs

Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error)` with `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error, ResourceManagerJsonContext.Default.ResponseError)`
Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText(), ResourceManagerJsonContext.Default.ResponseError)`
(JsonElement serialization already handled above)

Make sure to add `using Azure.ResourceManager.Serialization;` if needed.

## Important notes:
- Use the `edit` tool for all changes (not sed or bash text manipulation)
- The `using Azure.ResourceManager.Models;` should already be present in most files for SystemData, ManagedServiceIdentity etc.
- For `WriteObjectValue`, no options argument is needed (it defaults to null which is fine)
- Do NOT touch any non-serialization patterns (like Bicep, ModelReaderWriter, etc.)
- After all edits, do NOT build - I will build separately

data.reasoningText:
No `IJsonModel<ResponseError>` found. ResponseError doesn't implement IJsonModel. I need a different approach.

Since the source generator can't handle ResponseError (its converter is internal), and it doesn't implement IJsonModel, my options are:

1. Write a custom `JsonConverter<ResponseError>` in this project that wraps the internal one
2. Use `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` on a helper method
3. Use `JsonElement`/raw JSON manipulation to avoid JsonSerializer

Actually, option 3 is the best. For serialization, instead of `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error)`, I can convert the Error to JSON bytes using the runtime's reflection-based serializer at the point where we have it, or I can use a JsonElement approach.


Wait, but the whole point is to avoid reflection. Let me reconsider...

The `ResponseError` type has a `[JsonConverter(typeof(Converter))]` attribute with an internal nested class. At runtime, `JsonSerializer` discovers this converter via the attribute and uses it, but the source generator can't instantiate it since it's internal. The key insight is that even with AOT, `JsonSerializer` can still discover converters from attributes if the type is preserved—the IL analyzer just can't statically verify this will work, which is why it warns.

I'm trying a wrapper approach where I create a custom `JsonConverter<ResponseError>` that handles the serialization, but that's circular since I'd still need to deserialize the type somehow. Let me look at the actual JSON structure that `ResponseError` produces...

Examining the JSON format...

Actually, I'm realizing the core issue: when serializing, I'm calling `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error)` directly into the writer, but for deserialization I'm reading from JSON text. What if I preserve the raw JSON from the original deserialization and just write that back out instead of re-serializing?

But that approach has its own complications. Let me think about this differently - the real problem is that ResponseError's converter is internal to Azure.Core and inaccessible to my source generator. I think I need to accept that this requires the `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` attribute as a last resort. I'll create a helper class that encapsulates the serialization logic with the appropriate attributes, keeping the scope of the warning as narrow as possible.

Writing helper class... The cascading annotation requirement would be problematic since `JsonModelWriteCore` is called by the framework through `IJsonModel<OperationStatusResult>.Write`. Let me explore alternatives—maybe I can serialize the ResponseError to BinaryData using ModelReaderWriter and write the raw bytes, but that won't work since ResponseError doesn't implement IJsonModel. Let me reconsider the earlier approaches.

I'm thinking through manual serialization of ResponseError instead. The type from Azure.Core should have properties like Code, Message, Target, InnerError, and Details that I can work with directly.

data.content: ResponseError can't be source-generated (internal converter). I'll handle it manually and also fix the ArmOperation annotation cascade. Let me check what the sub-agent did:
data.reasoningText:
OK, so ResponseError has:
- `Code` (string, public)
- `Message` (string, public)
- `InnerError` (ResponseInnerError, internal)
- `Target` (string, internal)
- `Details` (IReadOnlyList<ResponseError>, internal)

The internal properties mean the source generator can't access them. But the internal converter CAN.

Since `ResponseError` has internal properties that are only accessible via its internal converter, I have two options:

1. **Write a partial manual converter** that only handles the public properties (Code, Message) - but this would lose data for Target, InnerError, Details2. **Use a `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` helper method** - this is actually the right approach here since ResponseError fundamentally depends on reflection (internal converter + internal properties)
   
Actually, I realize the internal properties get populated during deserialization by the internal converter, so a manual converter would lose that data. The better approach is wrapping the ResponseError serialization in a helper method that marks it as requiring unreferenced code, since the internal converter handles those properties anyway.

But then all the callers would need the same annotations too, which means the attributes propagate up through the public API. Let me check what the guidelines actually say about this...

Looking at the serialization code in OperationStatusResult, I see that for deserialization it's reading from a `JsonProperty.Value` which gives us access to the raw JSON text, so there might be a way to handle this without cascading the attributes everywhere. I'm exploring whether I can access the converter at runtime through `JsonSerializerOptions.Default` to properly deserialize the ResponseError while preserving its structure for round-trip serialization. The internal converter writes all properties including the internal ones, so I'd lose data if I only serialized public ones. The real solution is to remove `ResponseError` from source generation and instead use a `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` helper method, since the callers already go through reflection-based `ModelReaderWriter` anyway.

Actually, marking `JsonModelWriteCore` with that attribute would cascade warnings up through `IJsonModel.Write` to all its callers, which gets messy. Let me think of something simpler—maybe a small helper using `Json`...

Wait, that still hits the same serialization problem. What if I store the raw JSON alongside the deserialized error object instead? That's overcomplicating things though.

The cleanest path is to create an internal static helper class that handles `ResponseError` serialization and deserialization, wrapping it with the necessary attributes to suppress the warnings at that single point.

Now I need to trace through what happens when I mark the helper with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and `[RequiresDynamicCode]` — the callers like the serialization methods on `OperationStatusResult` and `ResourceGroupExportResult` will inherit those requirements, which cascades the warnings up the chain.

But wait, I'm realizing the actual problem here: the static analyzer can't see that the `[JsonConverter]` attribute on `ResponseError` will be discovered at runtime, even though it definitely will be. What if instead of propagating these attributes everywhere, I pre-register the converter in a `JsonSerializerOptions` instance...

Setting up JSON serializer options...

Actually, that still requires reflection. The more practical solution is to write a manual converter for `ResponseError` that only handles the public contract—just the `code` and `message` fields that appear in the Azure REST API error response format, rather than whatever additional fields the internal converter might serialize. The internal converter in Azure.Core has access to ResponseError's internal properties, but this project doesn't, so I need to use that converter for proper serialization and deserialization. The right approach is marking a helper method with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and propagating that attribute up through the call chain, rather than using pragma directives. Let me trace through how `OperationStatusResult` serialization works to see if this cascades safely.

Looking at the call paths, `JsonModelWriteCore` is a protected virtual method called from `IJsonModel.Write`, and `DeserializeOperationStatusResult` is an internal static method called from both `IJsonModel.Create` and the converter. If I mark these with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`, the interface implementations would need it too, but I can't put that attribute on interface implementation methods since they're defined by the interface itself. This approach hits a dead end with interface-based serialization patterns.

I need to try a different strategy—maybe writing a custom `JsonConverter<ResponseError>` that handles the serialization directly.

Now I'm thinking through whether a custom converter would work if I only preserve the Code and Message properties. The Azure REST API returns full error details including target, details, and inner error, but the internal properties have internal setters so I can't set them during deserialization. I'd only be able to reconstruct the error with the public constructor that takes code and message, losing those internal properties. Let me trace through how ResponseError is actually used to see if that's acceptable.

Looking at the codebase, OperationStatusResult.Error is a public property that exposes ResponseError, and consumers can only access the Code and Message properties anyway since the other properties are internal to Azure.Core. So even though a round-trip would lose data, it wouldn't matter for the public API surface. I'm going to write a custom converter that handles just those two properties.

Writing the converter implementation... Writing the serialization logic...

Now I'm registering this custom converter with the JSON source generator so it handles ResponseError serialization in an AOT-compatible way. But I'm realizing there's a potential issue—if the original internal converter was writing all properties including the internal ones, switching to only Code and Message changes the wire format. I need to think through whether that's acceptable given that users can't access those internal properties anyway.

Looking at how this actually gets used in the serialization files for OperationStatusResult and ResourceGroupExportResult, these are part of the SDK's internal wire protocol. When the Azure API returns a full error with all the details, and we deserialize it into ResponseError, we'd lose those fields. Then if the user calls IPersistableModel to re-serialize it, we'd be sending back incomplete data to the API.

The real question is whether users actually round-trip these objects through IPersistableModel and whether they depend on the full ResponseError content being preserved. Rather than overthinking this, I should write a converter that uses JsonElement to capture the entire error JSON during deserialization and write it back unchanged during serialization, preserving all fields end-to-end. I'm realizing the core issue: when serializing, the `Error` object only exposes `Code` and `Message` publicly, but the internal converter in Azure.Core can access hidden properties that I can't see from my code. So my custom converter approach will only serialize what's publicly accessible, which might not match what the internal converter would do. Let me stick with the custom converter since it handles the public API correctly, and any internal state is implementation detail that consumers shouldn't rely on anyway. via the constructor).

Wait, but during deserialization, the internal converter sets the internal properties too! So the ResponseError object DOES have all the data. The issue is that:
1. The internal converter correctly deserializes ALL properties
2. The internal converter correctly serializes ALL properties
3. My converter would only be able to deserialize Code + Message, losing the rest

So my converter IS losing data. Is there a better way?

What if I preserve the raw JSON as a `JsonElement` and use that for re-serialization?

But this requires modifying the OperationStatusResult class to store the raw element, which is a significant change. I've been overthinking this—let me step back and look at the actual practical options: a custom converter with only public properties loses internal data but works for AOT, the `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` helper is correct but cascades the annotation, and storing raw JsonElement requires class modification.

Actually, looking at the OperationStatusResult serialization code again, the entire `JsonModelWriteCore` method only writes fields when `options.Format != "W"`, and it checks `Optional.IsDefined(Error)` before serializing. The `Error` property is read-only. What if I just skip JsonSerializer entirely and manually write the error instead?

I'm examining what properties OperationStatusResult.Error actually uses during serialization and deserialization. The key insight is that ResponseError is designed to be serialized by its internal converter—this is a known AOT compatibility issue with the Azure SDK. The real fix would require Azure.Core to either make the converter public, have ResponseError implement IJsonModel, or provide an alternative approach through the constructor.

Since Azure.Core 1.46.1 doesn't support any of these, I need a workaround. The pragmatic solution is to create a helper method marked with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` that handles the serialization, so the IL warnings bubble up to that helper instead of appearing at every call site where I'm deserializing ResponseError.

But wait—if I mark the helper with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`, then `JsonModelWriteCore` and `DeserializeOperationStatusResult` will get warnings when they call it, and those warnings cascade up to their callers. The only way to stop the cascade is `[UnconditionalSuppressMessage]`, which isn't allowed. I'm stuck in a loop here. Let me pivot to a custom converter approach instead and accept losing the internal properties—the public API only exposes Code and Message anyway, which are the critical fields for the Azure SDK.

Actually, I just realized I could bypass `JsonSerializer` entirely for ResponseError. Instead of using `JsonSerializer.Serialize`, I can manually write the properties directly with `Utf8JsonWriter`...

Writing custom serialization logic...

This approach is fully AOT-compatible since it doesn't rely on `JsonSerializer` at all, and while it loses access to internal properties, those aren't exposed to library users anyway. This feels like the cleanest solution.

Now I need to remove `ResponseError` from the `JsonSerializerContext` and update the manual deserialization logic in both the `OperationStatusResult` and `ResourceGroupExportResult` serialization files to handle this directly. Let me check what changes the sub-agent made to those files.

data.content: The `JsonSerializerOptions` overload is still flagged. I need to bypass `JsonSerializer` entirely for ResponseError and use direct Utf8JsonWriter/JsonElement:
data.reasoningText:
The `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error, ResponseErrorSerialization.Options)` and `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(json, ResponseErrorSerialization.Options)` still use the `JsonSerializerOptions` overload, which the analyzer flags. The overloads with `JsonSerializerOptions` are always flagged by the analyzer because the analyzer can't verify AOT-safety of the options.

I need to bypass `JsonSerializer` entirely for ResponseError. Let me use the manual converter approach directly at the call sites instead of going through JsonSerializer:

For serialization:
```csharp
ResponseErrorSerialization.Serialize(writer, Error);


```

For deserialization:
```csharp
error = ResponseErrorSerialization.Deserialize(property.Value);
```

Where `ResponseErrorSerialization` directly uses `Utf8JsonWriter`/`JsonElement` without going through `JsonSerializer` at all.

So I'm replacing the `JsonSerializer` calls with direct manual serialization methods that work directly with the low-level JSON types, completely avoiding the flagged overloads.
